text: The hyperscopic video captures the removal of a teratoma from the right ovary, showing the surgical instruments delicately excising the mass from the surrounding ovarian tissue.

The liver and gallbladder are visible in the surgical field as the patient is positioned supine. The surgical team adjusts the patient to the Trendelenburg position, tilting her head downwards to improve visibility of the pelvis and move the omentum and intestines out of the way.
The patient is positioned with her head slightly elevated, revealing the uterus and both ovaries. The right ovary, which contains a teratoma, is visible but not prominently displayed at this moment.
The surgeon introduces additional laparoscopic trocars, carefully avoiding the inferior epigastric artery and vein, which are clearly visible in the laparoscopic view.

The surgeon introduces a laparoscopic trocar, ensuring it is positioned far from the visible inferior periosteic vessels to avoid injury. The same procedure is performed on the left side, with another trocar inserted away from these vessels.

The Da Vinci robotic system is docked, providing a magnified view of the right ovary. The surgeon examines the ovary, identifying the teratoma, which occupies a significant portion of the ovary, approximately five centimeters in size.
The surgeon uses endoshears to make a superficial incision on the right ovary, carefully avoiding penetration into the capsule of the teratoma to prevent inflammation from its liquid contents.
The surgeon carefully detaches the teratoma capsule from the ovarian capsule, attempting to avoid entering the teratoma. The teratoma capsule appears very thin during this process.

The surgeon inadvertently enters the teratoma, revealing yellow, thick fluid. A suction irrigator is introduced to aspirate the fluid, aiming to reduce inflammation and prevent the escape of cyst contents such as hair, cartilage, or other tissues.

The surgeon irrigates the pelvis multiple times using a suction irrigator to remove any residual content. The scissors, assisted by the Marilyn forceps, easily detach the teratoma capsule from the ovary.

After the cyst is completely removed, the surgeon uses a suction irrigator to clear the pelvis of any remaining liquid content. The ovary is irrigated, and areas of bleeding are addressed using Maryland forceps to achieve hemostasis, ensuring minimal injury to the remaining ovarian capsule.
The surgeon performs chromopertubation by introducing dye into the uterus, which visibly exits through both fallopian tubes, confirming their patency and openness.

The surgeon uses a suction irrigator to clear the area, then moves the laparoscopic camera to a different port. An endopoutra bag is introduced through the umbilical port to encapsulate the teratoma cyst for intact removal through the belly button.
